prompt: Rosa Parks was arrested in December 1955 for refusing to give up her seat on a bus in which American city?
markScheme: B — Rosa Parks was arrested on 1 December 1955 in Montgomery, Alabama. Her arrest sparked the Montgomery Bus Boycott, which lasted 381 days.

prompt: The 'I Have a Dream' speech was delivered by Martin Luther King Jr at which event in 1963?

options.label: C
options.text: The March on Washington for Jobs and Freedom

options.label: D
options.text: The founding of the SCLC in Atlanta

markScheme: C — King delivered the speech on 28 August 1963 at the Lincoln Memorial, Washington D.C., at the March on Washington for Jobs and Freedom, attended by approximately 250,000 people.

prompt: Japan invaded and occupied Manchuria in 1931 and subsequently set up which puppet state?
markScheme: A — Japan established the puppet state of Manchukuo in 1932, nominally ruled by the last Qing emperor Puyi, but effectively controlled by the Japanese Kwantung Army.

prompt: Which event in March 1938 saw Germany absorb Austria into the Third Reich without military resistance?
markScheme: A — The Anschluss (union) of Germany and Austria took place on 12–13 March 1938 when German troops entered Austria. The union was prohibited under the Treaty of Versailles but Britain and France took no action.

prompt: The Voting Rights Act of 1965 was most directly triggered by violent events at which location?

options.label: A
options.text: Birmingham, Alabama — the use of fire hoses against protesters

options.label: B
options.text: Selma, Alabama — 'Bloody Sunday' on the Edmund Pettus Bridge

options.label: C
options.text: Little Rock, Arkansas — the school desegregation crisis

options.label: D
options.text: Greensboro, North Carolina — attacks on sit-in protesters

markScheme: B — On 7 March 1965, state troopers attacked peaceful marchers at the Edmund Pettus Bridge in Selma. The televised violence shocked the nation and directly prompted President Johnson to introduce the Voting Rights Act.

prompt: Neville Chamberlain's policy of appeasement towards Nazi Germany is BEST described as:

options.label: A
options.text: Seeking to contain German expansion through military alliances

options.label: B
options.text: Making concessions to Hitler in the hope of satisfying his ambitions and preserving peace

options.label: C
options.text: Imposing economic sanctions to weaken Germany's rearmament programme

options.label: D
options.text: Building a military alliance with the Soviet Union to deter German aggression

markScheme: B — Appeasement involved making concessions to Hitler's demands (e.g. Munich Agreement, 1938) in the belief that satisfying his grievances would prevent another European war. It was widely discredited after Germany occupied all of Czechoslovakia in March 1939.

prompt: Describe two methods used by civil rights activists during the sit-in movement of 1960.
markScheme:
For each method — 1 mark for identification, 1 mark for development:

Method 1 — Nonviolent occupation of lunch counters:
• Activists (often students) would sit at 'whites-only' lunch counters and refuse to leave until served or arrested
• Even when verbally abused or physically attacked, protesters maintained discipline and nonviolence [2]

Method 2 — Organised mass participation:
• Protests spread rapidly from Greensboro (1 February 1960) to dozens of cities across the South
• SNCC coordinated the movement and trained activists in nonviolent resistance techniques [2]

Additional credit for: economic boycotts of segregated businesses; media strategy to attract national coverage; legal challenges to arrests
Max 6 marks

prompt: Describe the main events of Japanese expansion in Asia between 1931 and 1941.
markScheme:
• Mukden Incident (September 1931): staged explosion used as pretext to occupy Manchuria [1]
• Creation of Manchukuo puppet state (1932) under Puyi; League of Nations condemned but took no action [1]
• Japan withdrew from League of Nations (1933) after Lytton Commission report [1]
• Marco Polo Bridge Incident (July 1937) sparked full-scale Sino-Japanese War [1]
• Nanjing Massacre (December 1937): Japanese forces killed estimated 100,000–300,000 Chinese civilians [1]
• Expansion into Southeast Asia (Indochina 1940–41); US oil embargo (1941) prompted attack on Pearl Harbor (December 1941) [1]
Max 6 marks

prompt: Explain the importance of the Civil Rights Act of 1964 for African Americans.
markScheme:
• Outlawed discrimination in public accommodations (restaurants, hotels, theatres) — ended legal basis for Jim Crow [1]
• Title VII prohibited employment discrimination on grounds of race, colour, religion, sex, or national origin [1]
• Created Equal Employment Opportunity Commission (EEOC) to investigate complaints [1]
• Gave federal government power to withhold funding from programmes practising discrimination [1]
• Validated years of nonviolent protest — movement's goals achieved through legislation [1]
• However, did not address voting rights (tackled by Voting Rights Act 1965) or housing discrimination [1]
Max 6 marks

prompt: Describe the key events of German expansion in Europe between 1936 and 1939.
markScheme:
• Remilitarisation of the Rhineland (March 1936): German troops entered the demilitarised zone; France and Britain did not respond militarily [1]
• Anschluss with Austria (March 1938): German troops entered Austria; union declared despite prohibition in Treaty of Versailles [1]
• Munich Agreement (September 1938): Britain, France, Italy and Germany agreed to transfer Sudetenland to Germany; Czechoslovakia not consulted [1]
• Occupation of remaining Czechoslovakia (March 1939): Germany seized Bohemia-Moravia, making clear expansion was not limited to ethnic Germans [1]
• German demands on Poland over Danzig and the Polish Corridor (1939); Britain and France gave Poland a guarantee of independence [1]
• Invasion of Poland (1 September 1939): triggered British and French declarations of war [1]
Max 6 marks

prompt: Explain why the Little Rock Crisis of 1957 was important for the Civil Rights Movement in the United States.
markScheme:
• Governor Orval Faubus used Arkansas National Guard to prevent nine Black students from entering Central High School, defying Brown v Board ruling [1]
• President Eisenhower sent 101st Airborne Division to enforce desegregation — first use of federal troops in South since Reconstruction [1]
• Demonstrated federal government could and would enforce Supreme Court rulings against state resistance [1]
• 'Little Rock Nine' became symbols of courage and the costs of desegregation [1]
• Crisis broadcast internationally, embarrassing the US during Cold War [1]
• Showed limits of peaceful compliance — even moderate steps faced violent opposition, radicalising some activists [1]
Max 6 marks

prompt: Explain the methods used by the Civil Rights Movement between 1955 and 1965 and assess how successful they were.
markScheme:
Use IB Paper 2 markbands (0–15). SL essays should demonstrate clear knowledge and some analysis.

Methods:
• Legal challenges: NAACP litigation — Brown v Board (1954), Browder v Gayle (1956)
• Economic boycotts: Montgomery Bus Boycott — 381 days; economic pressure on city businesses
• Sit-ins: Greensboro (1960) — spread to over 50 cities; highlighted absurdity of segregation laws
• Freedom Rides (1961): tested enforcement of desegregation rulings in interstate transport
• Mass marches: Birmingham (1963), March on Washington (1963), Selma (1965)
• Appeals to federal government: King's relationship with Kennedy and Johnson

Successes:
• Brown v Board ruled segregation unconstitutional
• Montgomery Boycott desegregated city buses
• Civil Rights Act 1964 — ended legal discrimination in public life
• Voting Rights Act 1965 — enfranchised millions of Black voters

Limitations:
• De facto segregation in North persisted
• Economic inequality not addressed
• Continued violence against Black Americans

Level 4–5 (11–15): clear argument, specific evidence, some evaluation of success

prompt: Explain why Germany was able to expand its territory in Europe between 1936 and 1939 without triggering a major military response from Britain and France.
markScheme:
Use IB Paper 2 markbands (0–15).

Key factors:
• Policy of appeasement — belief that satisfying legitimate grievances would prevent war
• Memory of WWI — public in Britain and France deeply opposed to another major conflict
• Military unpreparedness — British forces not ready for continental war; RAF still rearming
• Economic constraints — Depression limited defence spending
• Mistaken assessment of Hitler — Chamberlain believed he was a rational statesman
• Belief in legitimacy of some German claims — ethnic Germans in Austria and Sudetenland
• British Empire — vulnerability of overseas territories reduced appetite for European commitments
• Munich Agreement (1938) — each concession emboldened Hitler to demand more
• Failure of collective security — League of Nations ineffective; no working alliance with USSR

Consequences:
• March 1939 — Hitler occupied all of Czechoslovakia, proving appeasement had failed
• Britain and France issued guarantee to Poland; declared war September 1939

Level 4–5 (11–15): clear explanation of multiple factors with specific evidence

prompt: Describe the key events of the US Civil Rights Movement between 1955 and 1965, and explain what they achieved.
markScheme:
Use IB Paper 2 markbands (0–15). SL: clear narrative and explanation expected.

Key events:
• Montgomery Bus Boycott 1955–56 — desegregated city buses; elevated King
• Little Rock Crisis 1957 — federal enforcement of school desegregation
• Sit-ins 1960 — challenged lunch counter segregation; formed SNCC
• Freedom Rides 1961 — desegregated interstate travel facilities
• Birmingham Campaign 1963 — confronted Bull Connor; prompted Civil Rights Act
• March on Washington 1963 — 250,000 marchers; 'I Have a Dream'
• Civil Rights Act 1964 — outlawed discrimination in public life and employment
• Selma Marches 1965 — 'Bloody Sunday'; prompted Voting Rights Act
• Voting Rights Act 1965 — eliminated barriers to Black political participation

Achievements:
• Legal dismantling of Jim Crow system
• Constitutional guarantees enforced
• Foundation for further progress (housing, affirmative action)

Level 4–5 (11–15): accurate events with clear explanation of significance and achievement

prompt: Explain the causes of Japanese aggression in Asia during the 1930s.
markScheme:
Use IB Paper 2 markbands (0–15).

Economic causes:
• Japan lacked domestic raw materials; needed overseas resources for industrial economy
• Great Depression devastated exports and created political instability

Political/military causes:
• Ultranationalist ideology — belief in Japan's destiny to lead Asia
• Militarist factions and Kwantung Army acted independently of civilian government
• Weak civilian governments unable to control the military; assassinations removed moderates

International factors:
• League of Nations failed to act effectively — no sanctions after Manchuria
• Western powers distracted by domestic economic crises
• Success of Manchukuo emboldened further expansion

Key events as evidence:
• Manchuria (1931), Marco Polo Bridge/China (1937), Pearl Harbor (1941)

Level 4–5 (11–15): clear explanation of multiple causes with supporting evidence
